topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam on the page, a mocking scarlet stain across the meticulously calculated score. I stared at the 'D-'. It felt like a physical blow, a sudden, sharp jolt that made my hands tremble so violently I had to grip the desk edge until my knuckles were white. "This is impossible," I muttered, the sound tight and thin in the quiet library. I slammed my textbook shut, the sound echoing disproportionately loud in the silence. My jaw clenched so hard I tasted copper. Every perfect late night, every agonizing hour spent wrestling with those concepts, seemed to have dissolved into this single, humiliating mark. I wanted to rip the paper in half, to scream until my throat tore, but I forced myself to breathe, the air catching painfully in my chest. I stood up, pacing the narrow aisle, my footsteps heavy and uneven, a caged animal vibrating with suppressed fury.

[story 2]

My eyes scanned the grade sheet again, refusing to accept the numbers presented. A 'C+'? For the project I poured my entire soul into? The sheer audacity of it made my stomach churn. I felt a hot, furious pressure building behind my eyes, a relentless heat that made the fluorescent lights above seem to buzz with malice. I snatched the paper, crumpling it instantly into a tight, illegible ball before letting it drop onto the floor. I kicked it once, sharply, the sound a small, frustrated explosion. "Unbelievable," I hissed, my voice dangerously low, vibrating with contained intensity. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my fingers ached, watching the indifferent city rush by. Every passing car felt like an insult, a reminder of a world that didn't value the effort I had expended.

[story 3]

I reread the feedback, my eyes skipping over the polite, vague suggestions and zeroing in on the final tally. It was a deliberate miscalculation, a gross oversight that cost me dearly. A slow, cold wave washed over me, quickly followed by a surge of raw, blinding anger. I stood up so fast my chair scraped backward across the linoleum floor. I didn't speak; words felt inadequate, clumsy tools for this level of internal combustion. My breath came in ragged, shallow gasps. I walked to my desk, my movements jerky and sharp, and began methodically stacking my notes, each book placed with aggressive precision. I slammed my fist onto the desk—not hard enough to break anything, but with enough force to rattle the pens in the holder. The vibration traveled up my arm, a tangible manifestation of the sheer, suffocating injustice of it all.
